source: OpenGlaze chemistry rules with ceramic science caveats

rules.name: Iron 2% + oxidation = amber/tan
rules.description: Iron oxide at ~2% in an oxidation (oxygen-rich) atmosphere produces warm amber to tan colors.
rules.caveat: Color varies with base glaze chemistry and iron source (red iron oxide vs black iron oxide). Calcium-heavy bases shift toward yellow-tan; potassium-heavy bases allow warmer reds.

rules.name: Iron 2% + reduction = celadon green
rules.description: Iron oxide at ~2% in a reduction (oxygen-depleted) atmosphere produces the classic celadon green.
rules.caveat: Depth of green depends on reduction intensity. Light reduction = gray-green; heavy reduction = deeper green. Calcium promotes celadon color; high boron can wash it out.

rules.name: Copper 1% + oxidation = green
rules.description: Copper oxide at ~1% in oxidation produces reliable greens ranging from mint to forest green.
rules.caveat: Higher copper percentages deepen the green. Above ~3% may produce very dark green approaching black. Calcium fluxes produce turquoise-greens; sodium fluxes shift toward blue-green.

rules.name: Copper 1% + heavy reduction = copper red
rules.description: Copper oxide at ~1% in heavy reduction CAN produce copper red (sang de boeuf), but this is one of the most difficult and unpredictable effects in ceramics.
rules.conditions: Copper oxide 1%, heavy reduction atmosphere, controlled cooling, thick application
rules.outcome: Copper red to oxblood red — when it works
rules.caveat: ~50-60% failure rate for beginners, improving to 20-40% with experience. Requires: precise reduction timing (not too early or copper re-oxidizes), slow controlled cooling through 1500°F-1800°F, thick application (thin coats go green), specific base chemistry (low alumina, adequate silica). Many potters fire dozens of pieces before achieving consistent results. Consider this advanced.

rules.name: Cobalt 0.5-2% = blue in any atmosphere
rules.description: Cobalt oxide is among the most reliable ceramic colorants. Small amounts produce strong blues in most atmospheres, though magnesium can shift toward lavender and iron toward blue-green.
rules.outcome: Blue — from pale sky blue (0.5%) to deep cobalt blue (2%)
rules.caveat: Cobalt is very strong. Above 2% it tends toward black. Combined with iron it produces blue-greens. With magnesium it can produce lavender.

rules.name: Manganese 2-8% + oxidation = purple/brown
rules.description: Manganese dioxide at 2-8% in oxidation produces purples and browns. Lower percentages give purple, higher give brown.
rules.caveat: Manganese can become volatile at cone 6+ temperatures, potentially affecting nearby pieces. Use ventilation.

rules.name: Manganese + reduction = metallic brown/black
rules.description: Manganese in reduction produces metallic browns and blacks. The reduction atmosphere changes the color significantly compared to oxidation.
rules.caveat: WARNING: Manganese fumes are toxic during firing. Always fire with adequate ventilation. Some studios avoid manganese entirely for health reasons.

rules.name: Chrome + tin = pink
rules.description: Chromium oxide combined with tin oxide produces pink. This is a classic ceramic color interaction.
rules.conditions: Chromium oxide + tin oxide in base glaze
rules.caveat: Very sensitive to proportions. Too much chrome = green; too much tin = white. The sweet spot is narrow. Chrome can also cause glaze defects (crawling, pinholing) in some bases. CRITICAL: Zinc in the base glaze will turn chrome-tin pinks brown. Use zinc-free bases for reliable pinks. Best results in oxidation. In reduction, chrome may reduce to darker compounds, muting the pink.

rules.name: Similar chemistry = compatible
rules.description: Glazes with similar base chemistry (same flux family, similar thermal expansion) tend to be compatible when layered.
rules.conditions: Two glazes with similar silica/alumina/flux ratios
rules.outcome: Low risk of defects (crawling, crazing, shivering)
rules.caveat: Similar chemistry is a guideline, not a guarantee. Application thickness and drying time between layers also matter.

rules.name: Shino over non-Shino = high risk crawling
rules.description: Shino glazes have high feldspar content and can crawl when applied over glazes with different chemistry. Risk level: MODERATE to HIGH depending on base glaze chemistry and application.
rules.outcome: MODERATE to HIGH risk of crawling (glaze pulls away from surface in patches)
rules.caveat: Some combinations do work, especially if the base glaze is also high in feldspar. Some potters intentionally chase crawl textures for aesthetic effect. If you want to try it: apply Shino thin, apply over a similar high-feldspar base, and accept that results will vary. NOT guaranteed to crawl.

rules.name: Zinc affects multiple colorants
rules.description: Zinc oxide in a base glaze interacts with several colorants, sometimes in unwanted ways. Understanding zinc interactions is important when choosing glazes to layer.
rules.conditions: Zinc oxide present in base glaze
rules.outcome: Chrome-tin pinks turn brown; copper color development can shift; iron greens may become lighter or browner
rules.caveat: Always check base glaze for zinc before applying chrome-tin pink glazes. Some popular clear glazes contain zinc and will ruin pinks.

rules.name: Crawling
rules.description: Crawling occurs when glaze pulls away from the clay body during firing, leaving bare patches. It's caused by poor adhesion between glaze and body, or incompatibility between layered glazes.
rules.conditions: Dust on bisque, greasy surface, incompatible layered glazes, glaze applied too thick, Shino over non-Shino, high clay content (especially Shinos), poor bisque prep (dust/oil)
rules.outcome: Bare patches where glaze has receded, sometimes with sharp edges
rules.caveat: Light crawling can be intentional and beautiful. Prevent by: cleaning bisque with damp sponge, letting each layer dry fully before applying next, keeping first layer thin, wiping bisque with damp sponge, thinner coats on Shinos.

rules.name: SiO2:Al2O3 ratio predicts surface
rules.description: The SiO2:Al2O3 ratio is the single most useful predictor of glaze surface character. Higher ratios favor gloss, lower ratios favor matte.
rules.outcome: Above 10: high gloss; 7-10: glossy; 4-7: satin; 1.5-4: matte; below 1.5: dry/underfired
rules.caveat: High-flux glazes (CaO, B2O3) may be glossy at lower ratios. Underfired glazes may appear matte regardless of ratio. Cooling rate affects surface development.

rules.name: Boron as a powerful flux
rules.description: Boron (from borax, gerstley borate, or frits) is a very powerful flux that lowers melting temperature significantly. It affects color development and can cause running at high temperatures.
rules.outcome: Lower melting temperature, potential color shifts, increased running risk
rules.caveat: Boron can turn some blue glazes green and affect chrome-based colors. High boron glazes may run more than expected. Common in many commercial low-fire and mid-range glazes.

rules.name: Food safety is combinational
rules.description: Food safety requires all three: safe chemistry + intact surface + proper firing. One weak link breaks the chain.
rules.conditions: Any glazed vessel intended for food or beverage contact
rules.outcome: Safe glaze: fully mature, smooth surface, no toxic leaching, passes acid test
rules.caveat: Matte surfaces from underfiring are the primary leaching risk. True matte glazes (high alumina, fully fired) can be food safe. The acid test (lemon juice, 24h) is the simplest at-home screening.
